nodes.question_text: Has the resident had any falls in the last 6 months?
nodes.context_hint: Fall history is the strongest single predictor of future falls. Per WAC 388-106-0095, pathological bone fracture (often fall-related) is a CARE clinical-complexity factor; locomotion ADL (WAC 388-106-0105) and CPS (WAC 388-106-0090) also drive composite fall risk.

nodes.question_text: How many falls in the last 6 months?
nodes.context_hint: Two or more falls in 6 months meets the operational threshold for elevated fall risk in most clinical guidelines and is worth capturing as a discrete risk-register entry.

nodes.question_text: Under what circumstances did the most recent fall occur?
nodes.context_hint: Toileting falls and night-time bedside falls have specific environmental countermeasures (bedside commode, low bed, motion-sensor lighting). Capture circumstance to drive a targeted care-plan intervention.

nodes.question_text: What was the worst outcome of any fall in the last 6 months?
nodes.context_hint: An ER visit or hospitalization from a fall raises both clinical and regulatory stakes. DSHS enforcement scrutiny on AFH fall management increases after a hospitalized fall, especially if unwitnessed.

nodes.question_text: What mobility or assistive device does the resident use?
nodes.context_hint: Device alone is not protective — residents using a walker who forget to use it (cognitive-mobility mismatch) are at higher risk than residents without a device who ambulate within their capacity. Capture compliance separately in gait stability.

nodes.question_text: How would you describe the resident's gait?
nodes.context_hint: ADL locomotion score (WAC 388-106-0105) reflects this assessment. Unsteady gait without assistive-device use is a high-risk mismatch and a capability-gap candidate if the AFH cannot provide constant supervision.

nodes.question_text: Does the resident take any medications known to elevate fall risk (psychotropics, sedatives/hypnotics, antihypertensives causing orthostasis, opioids, anticonvulsants)?
nodes.context_hint: Polypharmacy with 4+ fall-risk-increasing drugs (FRIDs) is a documented independent risk factor. Capture so synthesis can flag prescriber outreach for deprescribing review.

nodes.question_text: Which categories apply? (List all that apply.)
nodes.context_hint: Free-list rather than enum because residents often take multiple. Synthesis step will normalize.

nodes.question_text: What physical-environment accommodations will the AFH need to provide?
nodes.context_hint: AFH must mitigate environment-of-care hazards. Grab bars, low beds, perimeter-monitor alarms, and clear path-of-travel are common; tracking them at intake supports the negotiated care plan.

nodes.question_text: Has the resident had recent physical therapy involvement (currently active, recently discharged, or none)?
nodes.context_hint: An active PT plan is a coordination opportunity; a recent discharge with an unmet plan is a capability-gap concern. No PT after a fall typically indicates a missed referral.

nodes.question_text: Who is the PT provider, and what is the home-exercise or transfer-training plan the AFH should continue?
nodes.context_hint: Capture the provider name, frequency, and specific transfer-training or strengthening cues so AFH staff can reinforce between sessions. Continuity here is one of the highest-yield fall-prevention investments.
